fact: August 11 is exactly 223 days into the year, which means there are only 142 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is peak cricket chirping season - you can actually tell the temperature by counting cricket chirps for 14 seconds and adding 40!
The sun rises around 6 AM and sets around 8 PM in most of North America on this date, giving you about 14 hours of daylight to play outside.
August 11 falls in the middle of Leo season, when the zodiac lion is roaring loudest in the sky.
This is prime time for fireflies to put on their best light shows in backyards across the eastern United States.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, August 11 is deep winter, and some penguins are huddling together in groups of thousands to stay warm!
Sunflowers in the Northern Hemisphere are typically at their tallest and most dramatic around this date - some can tower over 12 feet high!
The moon phase on August 11 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it often appears extra orange because of summer humidity in the air.
In northern Canada and Alaska, the sun still sets super late around 10 PM, giving kids there almost 17 hours of daylight to have adventures.
August 11 is typically prime butterfly migration time in North America, when millions of monarchs start their epic 3,000-mile journey to Mexico.
The Earth is actually moving away from the sun right now compared to winter, but it feels hotter in the Northern Hemisphere because of the way our planet tilts!
